*Projection based on 2023 prison population. This and other figures regarding people past parole eligibility includes people in prison with sentences of more than one year who have not already been released on parole and excludes people with life sentences.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Wyoming, sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence, with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days per month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.²
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility Most recent data shows that 40 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 30 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 30 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will remain around 39 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 75 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 88 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 49 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison past parole eligibility were rape or sexual assault offenses (30 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variables series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years' reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) We then used the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. This is needed because some in several states, people who should have a PEY were missing it. We replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous years' reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (90 percent for those who's most serious offense was non-violent, and 90 for those whose most serious offense was violent).Third, in New York, parole only exists for non-violent offenses with statutorily maximum sentences of at least 3 years. Thus, we assumed that everyone with a sentence of less than 3 years and who does not already have PEY is not parole eligible. Fourth, most drug offenses are not eligible for parole, so no PEY was imputed for people with those offenses. However, even before our estimate, the raw NCRP data for New York indicated an inflated number of people past PEY compared to a New York report that, on Jan. 1 2018, 60 percent of people were not parole eligible. Additional analyses suggests that New York submitted not the discretionary PEY but the time at which people could be released on good time allowance or merit time allowance. Therefore, for New York, we only used the imputation and discarded the reported and the one obtained from matching to previous years' reports, and only used the imputation.⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state's eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation. If a state was missing data in less cases than the previously mentioned thresholds any year but the missing data substantially bias the estimated percent past parole eligibility, that year was excluded. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state's rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded.No year had to be excluded for this reason for Wyoming.

Data was excluded for years where more than 33 percent of the relevant cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. Relevant cases exclude those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures involving PEY were calculated among relevant cases only.Some years had to be excluded for this reason for Wyoming.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 were based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounted for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID-19 policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023jursdiction relevant population times the percentage of people past their PEY. The relevant population in 2023 was estimated based on a regression of the percentage of the total population represented by the relevant population between 2010 and 2020. For 2010–2022, the jursdiction population reported in the NPS was used.⁵
## Endnotes ¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Ni Zhang, and Edward E. Rhine, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Wyoming (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2017), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/wyoming_parole_profile.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/wyoming_parole_profile.pdf), 13.

² Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Wyoming (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2022), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/wyoming_doi_report_8_4_22.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/wyoming_doi_report_8_4_22.pdf), 7.

³ US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, [https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38](https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38).

⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., {it:American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report} (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf), 36-43;Reitz, , Rhine, {it:Prison-Release Discretion}, .

⁵ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, [https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1](https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1).“Wyoming Department of Corrections Population Report for Quarter Ending 2023Q4,” Wyoming Department of Corrections, accessed October 1, 2024, [https://drive.google.com/file/d/1R2octURWUtZ9MUPR4nVsHBPyS0j8RwGp/view.](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1R2octURWUtZ9MUPR4nVsHBPyS0j8RwGp/view.) Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 1 percent, changing from 2,112 in 2010 to 2,123 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 74 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 89 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 32 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 52 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 48 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison were drug offenses (24 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison increased 49 percent, from 747 in 2010 to 1,110 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In the most recent year of data available, 61 percent of parole-eligible people released in Wyoming were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 79 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 79 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 74 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 82 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 39 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 31 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 69 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people released from prison were drug offenses (29 percent). ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Measuring Disparities Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex: (1\) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group,
(2\) the average number of years individuals spent behind bars past their parole eligibility date, including by offense type, and
(3\) the average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

## Disparities in People Incarcerated Past Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ```{=html}
Black, non-Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past their parole eligibility at the same rate as White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Wyoming.

``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_wyoming.png){width=1180} ```{=html} Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Wyoming.
Analysis of disparities for Hispanic people should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.
```

National Corrections Reporting Program, 2019 and CSG Justice Center estimates
### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_wyoming.png){width=1180} ```{=html} Men were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than women, when accounting for prison population sizes in Wyoming.
```

## Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. Black people and Hispanic people spent less time behind bars after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 2 months less past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 6 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Black people spent on average 1 year 10 months more in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ### Sex ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 1 year 1 month less past parole eligibility compared to men.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among other violent offenses offenses, where men spent on average 1 year 6 months more in prison compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` :::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. Black people and Hispanic people spent less time behind bars than White people. Black people spent on average 1 month less in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 5 months less in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent on average 11 months more in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ### Sex ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by sex among released individuals. Women released spent on average 8 months less in prison compared to men.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where men spent on average 6 years 5 months more in prison compared to women.

``` ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility:** This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "Wyoming" ---
